questions.prompt:
Read the passage and answer the question.
Tree Rings in the Dry Valley
When visitors look at the old juniper trees in Dry Valley, they usually notice twisted trunks and gray bark. Dr. Melissa Grant notices something else: a record of rain and drought. Grant studies tree rings, the thin circles of growth that form under a tree’s bark each year. In wet years, many trees add wider rings. In dry years, the rings are often narrower.
Grant and her team collected pencil-thin samples from several living junipers and fallen logs. The samples did not require cutting down any trees. In the lab, the team compared ring patterns from many trees to build a timeline that reached back more than two hundred years. A single tree can have an unusual year because of insects or damage, but matching patterns across many trees can reveal broader climate conditions.
The timeline showed that Dry Valley had experienced several long dry periods before modern weather stations existed. One drought in the late 1800s lasted longer than any drought recorded by local instruments. The finding did not mean that every future drought would be worse than the past, but it gave water planners a wider view of what the region had already survived.
Grant explained that tree rings are not a perfect record. Soil, slope, disease, and competition for sunlight can also affect growth. Still, when scientists compare many samples carefully, trees can help extend climate history beyond written records. The old junipers were not just surviving in Dry Valley; they were keeping a quiet account of it.
What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Tree rings can help scientists study past wet and dry periods beyond modern weather records.
choices.B: Tree rings prove that every future drought will be worse than past droughts.
choices.C: Dry Valley’s juniper trees must be cut down before they can be studied.
choices.D: Weather stations provide the only useful record of climate history.

questions.correctAnswerText: Tree rings can help scientists study past wet and dry periods beyond modern weather records.
questions.explanation: The passage explains how tree-ring patterns helped scientists extend Dry Valley’s climate timeline beyond modern instruments.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Tree Rings in the Dry Valley.”
Why did Grant’s team compare ring patterns from many trees?

choices.A: To identify broader climate patterns rather than rely on one tree’s unusual growth
choices.B: To decide which trees should be removed from the valley
choices.C: To prove that insects never affect tree growth
choices.D: To replace all modern weather instruments

questions.correctAnswerText: To identify broader climate patterns rather than rely on one tree’s unusual growth
questions.explanation: Comparing many trees helps scientists avoid drawing conclusions from one tree affected by local damage or insects.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “Tree Rings in the Dry Valley.”
Which TWO factors besides rainfall can affect tree growth?

choices.A: Soil
choices.B: Disease
choices.C: The color of weather station buildings
choices.D: The number of visitors in the valley
choices.E: The length of a scientist’s pencil

questions.correctAnswerText: Soil | Disease
questions.explanation: The passage lists soil, slope, disease, and competition for sunlight as factors that can affect growth.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Tree Rings in the Dry Valley.”
What can be inferred from the drought found in the late 1800s?

choices.A: The region experienced serious dry periods before modern instruments recorded local weather.
choices.B: Weather stations in Dry Valley have existed for more than one thousand years.
choices.C: The late 1800s drought prevented all trees from forming rings.
choices.D: Scientists can predict the exact date of every future drought.

questions.correctAnswerText: The region experienced serious dry periods before modern instruments recorded local weather.
questions.explanation: The late 1800s drought appeared in the tree-ring timeline even though it was not recorded by local instruments.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Tree Rings in the Dry Valley.”
In the sentence “the old junipers were not just surviving in Dry Valley; they were keeping a quiet account of it,” account most nearly means [D1].
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=record
questions.explanation: In context, account means a record of Dry Valley’s climate history.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Tree Rings in the Dry Valley.”
Match each target with the correct tile.

tiles.A: Wider rings often form in wet years, while narrower rings often form in dry years.
tiles.B: Samples from living trees and fallen logs created a timeline over two hundred years long.
tiles.C: Grant explains that soil, slope, disease, and sunlight competition can affect growth.
tiles.D: The team cut down every juniper in Dry Valley.

targets.T1: Evidence that rings can reflect climate conditions
targets.T2: Evidence that the study extended the time record
targets.T3: Evidence that tree-ring evidence has limits

questions.correctAnswerText: T1=Wider rings often form in wet years, while narrower rings often form in dry years.; T2=Samples from living trees and fallen logs created a timeline over two hundred years long.; T3=Grant explains that soil, slope, disease, and sunlight competition can affect growth.
questions.explanation: Each tile matches evidence about climate signals, time range, and limits.

questions.prompt:
Read the workplace document and answer the question.
Account Access Verification Procedure
To: Information Desk Staff, Department Assistants, and Supervisors
From: Technology Services Director
Subject: Account Access Verification Procedure
Beginning August 12, employees who request a password reset or account unlock must verify their identity before technology services changes access. This procedure applies to email, payroll, scheduling, inventory, and customer management accounts.
Employees requesting help in person must show a current employee badge or government-issued photo ID. Employees requesting help by phone must answer two verification questions already listed in the employee profile. Do not reset a password based only on a familiar voice, job title, or message from another employee.
If the employee cannot verify identity, create an Access Review Ticket and notify the supervisor listed in the system. Do not ask the employee to send a password, personal identification number, or security answer by email. Technology services will never request that information in a message.
The request record must include the employee name, department, account type, verification method, time of request, and initials of the staff member who completed the reset. This procedure is intended to prevent unauthorized access while helping employees return to work systems as quickly as possible.
What is the main purpose of the document?

choices.A: To explain how staff should verify identity before resetting or unlocking employee accounts
choices.B: To allow password resets based only on a familiar voice
choices.C: To require employees to email passwords to technology services
choices.D: To stop employees from using work accounts after August 12

questions.correctAnswerText: To explain how staff should verify identity before resetting or unlocking employee accounts
questions.explanation: The document explains identity verification rules for password resets and account unlocks.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Account Access Verification Procedure.”
What should staff do if an employee requesting help by phone cannot verify identity?

choices.A: Create an Access Review Ticket and notify the listed supervisor.
choices.B: Reset the password immediately because the employee called.
choices.C: Ask the employee to email a password.
choices.D: Unlock every account in the department.

questions.correctAnswerText: Create an Access Review Ticket and notify the listed supervisor.
questions.explanation: The procedure states that this must happen when an employee cannot verify identity.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “Account Access Verification Procedure.”
Which TWO details must be included in the request record?

choices.A: Account type
choices.B: Verification method
choices.C: The employee’s home address
choices.D: The employee’s favorite software program

questions.correctAnswerText: Account type | Verification method
questions.explanation: The request record must include account type and verification method, along with other information.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Account Access Verification Procedure.”
Why should staff not reset a password based only on a familiar voice or job title?

choices.A: Those details do not reliably prove the person’s identity.
choices.B: Familiar voices are never heard on phone calls.
choices.C: Job titles are not used anywhere in the workplace.
choices.D: Password resets are never allowed for employee accounts.

questions.correctAnswerText: Those details do not reliably prove the person’s identity.
questions.explanation: The document requires stronger verification than a familiar voice or job title.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Account Access Verification Procedure.”
Which sentence best explains the reason for the procedure?

choices.A: “Beginning August 12, employees who request a password reset or account unlock must verify their identity.”
choices.B: “Employees requesting help in person must show a current employee badge or government-issued photo ID.”
choices.C: “Technology services will never request that information in a message.”
choices.D: “This procedure is intended to prevent unauthorized access while helping employees return to work systems as quickly as possible.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “This procedure is intended to prevent unauthorized access while helping employees return to work systems as quickly as possible.”
questions.explanation: This sentence directly explains the purpose of the procedure.

questions.prompt:
Read the argument and answer the question.
Train New Supervisors Before Promotion Takes Effect
Companies should require new supervisors to complete basic leadership training before their promotion takes effect. Many employees are promoted because they are excellent at technical tasks, sales, scheduling, or customer service. Those skills matter, but supervising people also requires communication, documentation, feedback, conflict resolution, and fair decision-making.
Training can prevent common problems. A new supervisor may know the work well but still struggle to explain expectations, handle attendance issues, or give corrective feedback without sounding personal. A short training program can teach the supervisor how to document concerns, apply policies consistently, and ask for help before a situation becomes serious.
The requirement should be practical, not burdensome. Training could include a few short sessions, realistic workplace scenarios, and a checklist of resources. New supervisors could also meet with a mentor during the first month. The point is not to delay advancement unnecessarily. The point is to make sure the person starts with tools for leading people, not just knowledge of the job.
Some managers may argue that leadership is learned by experience. Experience is important, but unsupported trial and error can be costly for employees and the company. A person who is promoted into supervision should not have to guess how to handle responsibilities that affect other workers’ schedules, records, and morale.
What is the author’s main claim?

choices.A: Companies should train new supervisors before promotion because leading people requires skills beyond technical job knowledge.
choices.B: Companies should promote only employees who have never done technical work.
choices.C: New supervisors should learn every responsibility through unsupported trial and error.
choices.D: Leadership training should replace all workplace experience.

questions.correctAnswerText: Companies should train new supervisors before promotion because leading people requires skills beyond technical job knowledge.
questions.explanation: The author argues that supervision requires communication, documentation, feedback, and fair decision-making.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Train New Supervisors Before Promotion Takes Effect.”
Which evidence best supports the claim that technical skill alone may not be enough?

choices.A: Supervising people requires communication, documentation, feedback, conflict resolution, and fair decision-making.
choices.B: New supervisors could meet with a mentor during the first month.
choices.C: Some managers believe leadership is learned by experience.
choices.D: Training could include a few short sessions.

questions.correctAnswerText: Supervising people requires communication, documentation, feedback, conflict resolution, and fair decision-making.
questions.explanation: This evidence directly supports the claim that technical ability alone is not enough.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “Train New Supervisors Before Promotion Takes Effect.”
Which TWO training features does the author recommend?

choices.A: Realistic workplace scenarios
choices.B: A checklist of resources
choices.C: Promotion without preparation
choices.D: Ignoring company policies

questions.correctAnswerText: Realistic workplace scenarios | A checklist of resources
questions.explanation: The author recommends scenarios, a resource checklist, short sessions, and mentoring.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Train New Supervisors Before Promotion Takes Effect.”
How does the author respond to the idea that leadership is learned by experience?

choices.A: By arguing that experience matters but unsupported trial and error can be costly
choices.B: By saying experience has no value in leadership
choices.C: By claiming supervisors should never ask for help
choices.D: By suggesting that employees should not be promoted

questions.correctAnswerText: By arguing that experience matters but unsupported trial and error can be costly
questions.explanation: The author acknowledges experience but explains why training should come first.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Train New Supervisors Before Promotion Takes Effect.”
Choose the sentence that would best strengthen the author’s argument if added after paragraph 2: [D1]

D1: In an internal review, most early supervisor complaints involved unclear expectations, inconsistent documentation, or poorly handled feedback.
The training room has two clocks and a cabinet of binders.

questions.correct: D1=In an internal review, most early supervisor complaints involved unclear expectations, inconsistent documentation, or poorly handled feedback.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=In an internal review, most early supervisor complaints involved unclear expectations, inconsistent documentation, or poorly handled feedback.
questions.explanation: This evidence strengthens the argument by connecting training topics to actual supervisor problems.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Train New Supervisors Before Promotion Takes Effect.”
Which assumption is most important to the author’s argument?

choices.A: Basic leadership skills can be taught before a new supervisor begins managing others.
choices.B: Strong technical workers can never become good supervisors.
choices.C: Documentation is unrelated to supervision.
choices.D: Promotions should always be delayed for many years.

questions.correctAnswerText: Basic leadership skills can be taught before a new supervisor begins managing others.
questions.explanation: The argument depends on the idea that training can prepare supervisors for people-management responsibilities.

questions.prompt:
Read the literary excerpt and answer the question.
The Adoption Description
Andre had fed the dogs at Harbor Animal Rescue for six months, but he had never written an adoption description. That was usually Maya’s job. She knew how to turn “barks at delivery trucks” into “alert and enthusiastic,” and how to explain a shy dog without making the dog sound impossible to love.
On Thursday, Maya handed Andre a clipboard. “Write one for Juniper,” she said. “Be honest, but help people see her.”
Juniper was a brown dog with a white chin and careful eyes. She did not rush to the front of the kennel when visitors arrived. She watched first, then stepped forward if they crouched and waited. Andre wrote, “Juniper is nervous around people,” then crossed it out. It was true, but not complete.
He sat on the floor outside her kennel. Juniper leaned her shoulder against the gate. Andre started again: “Juniper likes slow introductions and quiet voices. Once she feels safe, she stays close and rests her chin on your knee.” He added that she would do best in a calm home and that she was still learning to walk past loud trucks.
The next day, a couple asked to meet “the dog who likes quiet voices.” They sat on the floor the way Andre suggested. Juniper watched, stepped forward, and placed her chin on one visitor’s knee.
Andre looked at the clipboard. He had not made Juniper sound easier than she was. He had made her easier to understand.
What is the main conflict in the excerpt?

choices.A: Andre must write an honest but helpful description of a shy shelter dog.
choices.B: Andre tries to convince Maya to stop helping at the animal rescue.
choices.C: Juniper refuses to meet any visitors under any conditions.
choices.D: The rescue decides to close before Juniper can be adopted.

questions.correctAnswerText: Andre must write an honest but helpful description of a shy shelter dog.
questions.explanation: The excerpt focuses on Andre learning how to describe Juniper accurately and usefully.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Adoption Description.”
Why does Andre cross out “Juniper is nervous around people”?

choices.A: The sentence is true but does not give a complete picture of Juniper.
choices.B: The sentence is false because Juniper is never cautious.
choices.C: Maya tells him not to mention Juniper’s behavior.
choices.D: Andre decides to write about another dog instead.

questions.correctAnswerText: The sentence is true but does not give a complete picture of Juniper.
questions.explanation: Andre realizes that “nervous” is accurate but incomplete.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Adoption Description.”
What does the couple’s request to meet “the dog who likes quiet voices” show?

choices.A: Andre’s description helped potential adopters understand how to approach Juniper.
choices.B: The couple misunderstood the description completely.
choices.C: Juniper’s description should have included only her color.
choices.D: Visitors are not allowed to sit on the floor at the rescue.

questions.correctAnswerText: Andre’s description helped potential adopters understand how to approach Juniper.
questions.explanation: The couple’s wording shows they understood the guidance in the description.

questions.prompt:

choices.A: She steps forward when visitors crouch and wait.
choices.B: She would do best in a calm home.
choices.C: She rushes at every visitor immediately.
choices.D: She refuses to rest near people.
choices.E: She is described as a gray cat.

questions.correctAnswerText: She steps forward when visitors crouch and wait. | She would do best in a calm home.
questions.explanation: These details reveal Juniper’s cautious nature and the environment she needs.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Adoption Description.”
What does the final sentence suggest about Andre’s writing?

choices.A: It balances honesty with understanding.
choices.B: It hides all of Juniper’s challenges.
choices.C: It makes Juniper sound like a different dog.
choices.D: It is meant only for rescue staff, not adopters.

questions.correctAnswerText: It balances honesty with understanding.
questions.explanation: Andre does not hide Juniper’s needs; he explains them in a way that helps adopters understand her.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Adoption Description.”
Match each target with the correct tile.

tiles.A: Andre writes “Juniper is nervous around people,” then crosses it out.
tiles.B: Andre explains that Juniper likes slow introductions and quiet voices.
tiles.C: Juniper places her chin on a visitor’s knee.
tiles.D: Andre writes that Juniper barks at every person.

targets.T1: Evidence that Andre rejects an incomplete description
targets.T2: Evidence that Andre gives useful guidance
targets.T3: Evidence that the guidance matches Juniper’s behavior

questions.correctAnswerText: T1=Andre writes “Juniper is nervous around people,” then crosses it out.; T2=Andre explains that Juniper likes slow introductions and quiet voices.; T3=Juniper places her chin on a visitor’s knee.
questions.explanation: The tiles show Andre revising, giving useful guidance, and seeing the description match Juniper’s behavior.

questions.prompt:
Read the informational passage and answer the question.
Understanding an Auto Insurance Deductible
An auto insurance deductible is the amount a policyholder must pay before the insurance company pays its part of a covered claim. If a repair after a covered accident costs $2,000 and the deductible is $500, the policyholder pays $500 and the insurer pays the remaining covered amount, subject to the policy terms.
A higher deductible usually lowers the monthly premium because the policyholder agrees to pay more if a claim occurs. A lower deductible usually raises the premium because the insurer may pay sooner. Choosing a deductible is not only about finding the lowest monthly cost. Drivers should consider whether they could afford the deductible after an unexpected accident.
Deductibles may also vary by type of coverage. Collision coverage, comprehensive coverage, and uninsured motorist property damage may have different deductibles. Some situations may not require a deductible at all, depending on the policy and state rules. This is why drivers should read the declarations page and ask questions before a claim happens.
A deductible does not decide whether a claim is covered. Coverage depends on the policy, the cause of damage, exclusions, and other conditions. Understanding the deductible helps drivers plan for out-of-pocket costs, but it is only one part of understanding auto insurance.
What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: An auto insurance deductible affects out-of-pocket costs and should be understood along with policy coverage.
choices.B: A deductible is the amount the insurance company pays before the driver pays anything.
choices.C: Drivers should always choose the highest deductible without considering savings.
choices.D: Deductibles determine whether every claim is covered.

questions.correctAnswerText: An auto insurance deductible affects out-of-pocket costs and should be understood along with policy coverage.
questions.explanation: The passage explains deductibles, premium trade-offs, coverage types, and limits.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Understanding an Auto Insurance Deductible.”
What happens when a covered repair costs $2,000 and the deductible is $500?

choices.A: The policyholder pays $500 before the insurer pays the remaining covered amount.
choices.B: The insurer pays only $500 and the policyholder pays nothing.
choices.C: The deductible disappears because the repair costs more than $1,000.
choices.D: The claim is automatically denied.

questions.correctAnswerText: The policyholder pays $500 before the insurer pays the remaining covered amount.
questions.explanation: The deductible is the amount the policyholder must pay before insurance pays its part.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Understanding an Auto Insurance Deductible.”
Why might a driver avoid choosing the highest deductible?

choices.A: The driver may not be able to afford that amount after an unexpected accident.
choices.B: A higher deductible always increases the monthly premium.
choices.C: A high deductible makes all repairs free.
choices.D: A deductible prevents drivers from reading a policy.

questions.correctAnswerText: The driver may not be able to afford that amount after an unexpected accident.
questions.explanation: The passage says drivers should consider whether they could afford the deductible after an accident.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Understanding an Auto Insurance Deductible.”
Which sentence best supports the idea that a deductible is only one part of insurance coverage?

choices.A: “A higher deductible usually lowers the monthly premium.”
choices.B: “Choosing a deductible is not only about finding the lowest monthly cost.”
choices.C: “Coverage depends on the policy, the cause of damage, exclusions, and other conditions.”
choices.D: “Drivers should read the declarations page and ask questions before a claim happens.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Coverage depends on the policy, the cause of damage, exclusions, and other conditions.”
questions.explanation: This sentence shows that the deductible is not the only factor in coverage.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Understanding an Auto Insurance Deductible.”
How is the passage mainly organized?

choices.A: It defines deductibles, explains cost trade-offs, and describes coverage limits.
choices.B: It tells a story about one driver buying a car.
choices.C: It ranks insurance companies from least to most expensive.
choices.D: It argues that drivers should never file insurance claims.

questions.correctAnswerText: It defines deductibles, explains cost trade-offs, and describes coverage limits.
questions.explanation: The passage defines the term, explains premium differences, and describes how deductibles relate to coverage.

questions.prompt:
Read the editing passage and choose the best words for the blanks.
Uniform Order Deadline
(1) Employees who need replacement uniforms should submit an order form by September 5. (2) The form should include shirt size, pant size, department, and employee number. (3) Supervisors use this information to approve orders before they are sent to the supplier. (4) [D1], late forms may not be included in the first shipment. (5) The approved order list [D2] reviewed by purchasing before the supplier receives it. (6) Employees should report size changes as soon as possible.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=Therefore; D2=will be
questions.explanation: “Therefore” connects the deadline to the result of late forms, and “will be reviewed” is grammatically correct.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Uniform Order Deadline.”
Which revision of sentence 6 is most concise while keeping the meaning?

choices.A: Employees should report size changes as soon as possible.
choices.B: Employees should make reports of changes in size at the soonest time possible.
choices.C: Size changes should be reported by employees in a way that happens soon.
choices.D: Employees, if size changes occur, should make reports of those size changes.

questions.correctAnswerText: Employees should report size changes as soon as possible.
questions.explanation: This sentence is concise and keeps the meaning.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses correct subject-verb agreement?

choices.A: The uniforms in the storage boxes needs new labels.
choices.B: The list of approved orders are on the supervisor’s desk.
choices.C: The employees who submit forms receive confirmation emails.
choices.D: The packet of size charts have been updated.

questions.correctAnswerText: The employees who submit forms receive confirmation emails.
questions.explanation: The plural subject “employees” correctly takes the plural verb “receive.”

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses the clearest pronoun reference?

choices.A: When Riley called Cameron after the fitting, she corrected the size.
choices.B: Riley called Cameron after the fitting, and Riley corrected the size.
choices.C: She corrected it when Riley called Cameron after the fitting.
choices.D: After the fitting, she called her and corrected it.

questions.correctAnswerText: Riley called Cameron after the fitting, and Riley corrected the size.
questions.explanation: Repeating “Riley” makes clear who corrected the size.

questions.prompt:
Choose the best transition for the blank.
The supplier discontinued one jacket style. [D1], purchasing selected a similar approved replacement.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=Therefore
questions.explanation: “Therefore” shows that purchasing selected a replacement because the original jacket style was discontinued.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is punctuated correctly?

choices.A: The first shipment arrived Monday: the remaining uniforms arrived Friday.
choices.B: The first shipment arrived Monday the remaining uniforms arrived Friday.
choices.C: The first shipment arrived Monday, the remaining uniforms arrived Friday.
choices.D: The first shipment arrived Monday: and the remaining uniforms, arrived Friday.

questions.correctAnswerText: The first shipment arrived Monday: the remaining uniforms arrived Friday.
questions.explanation: The colon correctly connects a complete sentence to a closely related follow-up detail.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is a complete sentence?

choices.A: Because the order form was late.
choices.B: After the supervisor approved the size chart.
choices.C: Purchasing sent the approved list to the supplier.
choices.D: While checking the uniform samples.

questions.correctAnswerText: Purchasing sent the approved list to the supplier.
questions.explanation: This is the only complete sentence.

questions.prompt: Which sentence maintains consistent verb tense?

choices.A: The supervisor reviewed the form and approves the order yesterday.
choices.B: The supervisor reviews the form and approved the order yesterday.
choices.C: The supervisor reviewed the form and approved the order yesterday.
choices.D: The supervisor had reviewed the form and approves the order yesterday.

questions.correctAnswerText: The supervisor reviewed the form and approved the order yesterday.
questions.explanation: Both verbs are in past tense and match “yesterday.”

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses parallel structure?

choices.A: The order form asks for shirt size, pant size, and whether the employee needs a jacket.
choices.B: The order form asks for shirt size, pant size, and jacket preference.
choices.C: The order form asks for sizing shirts, pant size, and to choose a jacket.
choices.D: The order form asks for shirt size, pants that are sized, and if a jacket is needed.

questions.correctAnswerText: The order form asks for shirt size, pant size, and jacket preference.
questions.explanation: The list uses parallel noun phrases.

questions.prompt:
Read the paired passages and answer the question.
Passage A: Use Standard Response Templates
The customer service department should use standard response templates for common questions. Customers often ask about shipping delays, return windows, warranty steps, appointment changes, and account updates. A well-written template can make sure each customer receives complete information, including deadlines, required documents, and next steps.
Templates can also reduce errors. When representatives are busy, they may forget to include an address, a link, a case number, or a warning about time limits. A template gives them a reliable starting point. Representatives can still personalize the greeting and add details specific to the customer’s situation. The goal is not to make every message sound identical; it is to make every message accurate.
The department should review templates regularly. Policies change, and outdated language can create confusion. Team leads could update templates after policy changes and ask representatives to report wording that customers find unclear. Used carefully, templates can save time while improving consistency.
Passage B: Let Representatives Write Individual Responses
The customer service department should not rely too heavily on standard response templates. Customers can tell when a message sounds copied, especially if it does not address the exact question they asked. A person who has already explained a problem may feel ignored if the response repeats general policy without recognizing the specific situation.
Individual responses can build trust. A representative who writes in their own words can show that they understood the customer’s concern, explain the reason for a decision, and choose details that fit the case. This is especially important when customers are frustrated or when the issue is unusual.
Templates can also lead to mistakes if representatives choose the wrong one or send it without editing. Instead of focusing on templates, the department should train employees to read carefully, explain policies clearly, and ask a supervisor when a case is complicated. Accuracy matters, but so does making customers feel heard.
What is the central difference between the two passages?

choices.A: Passage A supports templates for consistency and accuracy, while Passage B argues individual responses can better address specific customer concerns.
choices.B: Passage A argues that customers should stop asking questions, while Passage B argues that representatives should ignore policy.
choices.C: Passage A supports removing all deadlines, while Passage B supports canceling customer service.
choices.D: Passage A focuses on weather data, while Passage B focuses on animal shelters.

questions.correctAnswerText: Passage A supports templates for consistency and accuracy, while Passage B argues individual responses can better address specific customer concerns.
questions.explanation: Passage A emphasizes consistent complete information; Passage B emphasizes personalization and trust.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about customer service responses.
Which evidence most directly supports Passage A’s argument?

choices.A: Templates can help representatives remember deadlines, required documents, and next steps.
choices.B: Customers may feel ignored when a message sounds copied.
choices.C: Individual responses can show that a representative understood the concern.
choices.D: Representatives may choose the wrong template.

questions.correctAnswerText: Templates can help representatives remember deadlines, required documents, and next steps.
questions.explanation: This evidence directly supports Passage A’s claim that templates improve accuracy and completeness.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to the paired passages about customer service responses.
Which TWO claims from Passage B are supported with reasons or evidence?

choices.A: Copied-sounding messages may make customers feel ignored.
choices.B: Individual responses can help representatives address unusual issues.
choices.C: Templates always contain false information.
choices.D: Customers never need complete information.
choices.E: Representatives should avoid asking supervisors for help.

questions.correctAnswerText: Copied-sounding messages may make customers feel ignored. | Individual responses can help representatives address unusual issues.
questions.explanation: Passage B supports both claims as reasons to prefer individual responses.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about customer service responses.
Which statement from Passage A directly acknowledges that templates should not be used without adjustment?

choices.A: “The customer service department should use standard response templates for common questions.”
choices.B: “Representatives can still personalize the greeting and add details specific to the customer’s situation.”
choices.C: “A well-written template can make sure each customer receives complete information.”
choices.D: “Team leads could update templates after policy changes.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Representatives can still personalize the greeting and add details specific to the customer’s situation.”
questions.explanation: This sentence acknowledges that templates should be adjusted to fit the specific case.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about customer service responses.
Which statement is a supported inference from both passages?

choices.A: Both authors value accurate customer communication, but they disagree about how much structure representatives should use.
choices.B: Both authors believe customer service messages should never include policy details.
choices.C: Both authors think representatives should send identical messages in every case.
choices.D: Both authors argue that customer questions are not worth answering.

questions.correctAnswerText: Both authors value accurate customer communication, but they disagree about how much structure representatives should use.
questions.explanation: Both passages focus on improving communication, but they recommend different approaches.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about customer service responses.
Which additional evidence would most help evaluate both arguments?

choices.A: Data comparing response accuracy, customer satisfaction, resolution time, and error rates with templates and individual responses
choices.B: A list of representative desk locations
choices.C: A history of the company’s logo design
choices.D: A survey asking customers their favorite package colors

questions.correctAnswerText: Data comparing response accuracy, customer satisfaction, resolution time, and error rates with templates and individual responses
questions.explanation: These data would help evaluate the strengths and weaknesses of both methods.

questions.prompt:
Which revision fixes the misplaced modifier?
Packed in clear plastic sleeves, Maren counted the uniform forms before shipping.

choices.A: Packed in clear plastic sleeves, Maren counted the uniform forms before shipping.
choices.B: Maren counted the uniform forms packed in clear plastic sleeves before shipping.
choices.C: Maren, packed in clear plastic sleeves, counted the uniform forms before shipping.
choices.D: The plastic sleeves counted Maren’s uniform forms before shipping.

questions.correctAnswerText: Maren counted the uniform forms packed in clear plastic sleeves before shipping.
questions.explanation: This revision places the modifier next to “uniform forms,” the items packed in sleeves.

questions.prompt:
Which choice best combines the sentences without changing their meaning?
The supervisor sent a notice. The notice listed the uniform deadline.

choices.A: The supervisor sent a notice that listed the uniform deadline.
choices.B: The notice sent the supervisor and listed the uniform deadline.
choices.C: Listing the supervisor, the uniform deadline sent a notice.
choices.D: The supervisor sent, and the uniform deadline listed the notice.

questions.correctAnswerText: The supervisor sent a notice that listed the uniform deadline.
questions.explanation: This sentence clearly combines the ideas without changing the meaning.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses an apostrophe correctly?

choices.A: The employee’s uniform was placed in the correct department box.
choices.B: The employees uniform was placed in the correct department box.
choices.C: The employees’ uniform’s was placed in the correct department box.
choices.D: The employee uniforms’ was placed in the correct department box.

questions.correctAnswerText: The employee’s uniform was placed in the correct department box.
questions.explanation: The apostrophe correctly shows that the uniform belongs to one employee.

questions.prompt:
Read both passages. Write an evidence-based essay of 300–500 words explaining which position is better supported. Use evidence from both passages. Maintain a formal tone and organize your response clearly.
Passage A:
Require Airlines to Seat Young Children with Accompanying Adults
Airlines should be required to seat young children next to at least one accompanying adult without charging extra fees. Families often buy tickets together but later discover that available seats are scattered throughout the plane unless they pay for seat selection. A young child should not be separated from a parent or caregiver simply because the family did not pay an added fee.
The issue is not only comfort. A parent may need to help a child fasten a seat belt, follow safety instructions, manage anxiety, use medication, or respond during turbulence. Flight attendants are responsible for cabin safety, but they should not be expected to supervise children who could have been seated with their own adults from the start.
Airlines can still manage seating efficiently. The rule could apply to children under a certain age and require families to book together on the same reservation. It would not require every family member to sit together or guarantee preferred seats. It would simply ensure that one responsible adult is seated next to each young child.
Airlines already use complex systems to assign seats, sell upgrades, and manage boarding groups. Those systems should be able to prioritize basic child safety without turning it into an optional purchase. Families deserve clear protection from fees that affect a child’s supervision during a flight.
Passage B:
Do Not Create a New Airline Seating Requirement
Airlines should not face a broad new rule requiring free adjacent seats for families with young children. Seating on airplanes is limited, and many passengers have needs or preferences. Some travelers pay extra for aisle seats, window seats, extra legroom, or seats near companions. A new rule may force airlines to move other passengers or reduce the value of seats that customers already selected.
Airlines already have reasons to help families sit together when possible. Separating young children can create problems for crews and passengers, so many airlines try to fix seating before boarding or at the gate. A strict requirement may reduce flexibility by creating one rule for many different aircraft layouts, booking situations, and last-minute changes.
There is also a fairness concern. If families receive certain seat assignments at no charge, other passengers may ask why their needs do not receive the same treatment. Travelers with medical needs, elderly companions, or anxiety about flying may also have reasons to sit with someone. Airlines need room to balance many requests rather than follow a single automatic rule.
A better approach is clear disclosure. Airlines should tell families before purchase whether adjacent seats are available and what steps they can take. Regulators can require honesty without creating a seating rule that may be difficult to apply fairly.
Writing Task:
Which passage presents the better-supported argument about whether airlines should be required to seat young children next to an accompanying adult without extra fees? Write an essay explaining your position. Use evidence from both passages to support your analysis.
Rubric Guidance:
• Analyze which argument is better supported.
• Use relevant evidence from both passages.
• Explain why the evidence supports your claim.
• Address weaknesses or limitations in the less-supported argument.
• Organize ideas logically.
• Use clear sentence structure, grammar, and formal language.

questions.explanation: Extended Response answers should be reviewed using the rubric guidance. Strong responses make a clear claim, use evidence from both passages, explain why one argument is better supported, and maintain formal organization and language.
